   306‚Üí## Epic 3: Market Context & Price Chart
   307‚Üí
   308‚ÜíThe user gets the full market picture ‚Äî market cap, 24h range, and a 24h sparkline chart with hover tooltips ‚Äî completing the dashboard's information offering.
   309‚Üí
   310‚Üí### Story 3.1: Market Data Service (CoinGecko REST Polling)
   311‚Üí
   312‚ÜíAs a user,
   313‚ÜíI want the dashboard to fetch market data automatically in the background,
   314‚ÜíSo that I see up-to-date market cap, 24h change, and chart data without any action.
   315‚Üí
   316‚Üí**Acceptance Criteria:**
   317‚Üí
   318‚Üí**Given** the dashboard page loads and services initialize
   319‚Üí**When** the MarketDataService starts
   320‚Üí**Then** it fetches market cap, 24h change (percentage + absolute), 24h high/low, and hourly historical price data from CoinGecko REST API
   321‚Üí**And** `marketStore` is populated with a `MarketData` object: `{ marketCap, change24h, changePercent24h, high24h, low24h }`
   322‚Üí**And** `chartStore` is populated with an array of `ChartPoint` objects: `{ time: number, value: number }` for hourly data
   323‚Üí**And** `chartStore` is capped at 24 entries maximum ‚Äî oldest point shifted when appending new data
   324‚Üí**And** polling uses recursive `setTimeout` (not `setInterval`) with a 60-second interval (NFR11)
   325‚Üí**And** on CoinGecko API failure, last known store values are retained ‚Äî stores are never cleared (NFR12)
   326‚Üí**And** API errors are logged via `console.warn()` with endpoint and status code ‚Äî no user-facing error UI
   327‚Üí**And** timer references are stored and cleared before scheduling new timers (memory leak prevention)
   328‚Üí**And** `market-data-service.test.ts` covers data mapping, store updates, and error retention behavior

   373‚Üí## Epic 4: Connection Resilience & Reliability
   374‚Üí
   375‚ÜíThe dashboard never breaks ‚Äî WebSocket failures are handled silently with fallback sources, automatic reconnection, and clear stale data indication. The user can trust the data is always there.
   376‚Üí
   377‚Üí### Story 4.1: WebSocket Reconnection with Exponential Backoff
   378‚Üí
   379‚ÜíAs a user,
   380‚ÜíI want the dashboard to automatically recover from connection drops without my intervention,
   381‚ÜíSo that the live price feed resumes seamlessly after network interruptions.
   382‚Üí
   383‚Üí**Acceptance Criteria:**
   384‚Üí
   385‚Üí**Given** the ConnectionManager has an active Binance WebSocket connection
   386‚Üí**When** the WebSocket connection drops (error, close, or network failure)
   387‚Üí**Then** `statusStore` transitions to `'reconnecting'` (FR14)
   388‚Üí**And** reconnection attempts begin with exponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s, 30s cap (NFR10)
   389‚Üí**And** on successful reconnection, `statusStore` transitions back to `'live'`
   390‚Üí**And** the previous WebSocket instance is fully closed (`ws.close()`) and its reference nulled before creating a new connection (memory leak prevention, NFR17)
   391‚Üí**And** no JavaScript errors or white screens occur during disconnection or reconnection (NFR16)
   392‚Üí**And** Binance 24-hour connection expiry is handled as a standard disconnect/reconnect cycle ‚Äî no special logic (NFR13)
   393‚Üí**And** the last known price remains displayed in the Price Card during reconnection (NFR15)
   394‚Üí**And** `connection-manager.test.ts` covers backoff timing, listener cleanup, and reconnection state transitions
   395‚Üí
   396‚Üí### Story 4.2: CoinCap Fallback & REST Polling Fallback
   397‚Üí
   398‚ÜíAs a user,
   399‚ÜíI want the dashboard to try alternative data sources if the primary one fails,
   400‚ÜíSo that I always see price data regardless of which service is available.
   401‚Üí
   402‚Üí**Acceptance Criteria:**
   403‚Üí
   404‚Üí**Given** the ConnectionManager fails to connect or reconnect to Binance
   405‚Üí**When** Binance WebSocket is unavailable after reconnection attempts
   406‚Üí**Then** the ConnectionManager attempts to connect to CoinCap WebSocket at `wss://ws.coincap.io/prices?assets=bitcoin` (FR12)
   407‚Üí**And** CoinCap messages (`{ bitcoin: "68432.17" }`) are normalized to the same `PriceTick` format
   408‚Üí**And** on successful CoinCap connection, `statusStore` transitions to `'fallback'`
   409‚Üí**And** fallback from Binance to CoinCap completes without user intervention (NFR14)
   410‚Üí**And** if both Binance and CoinCap WebSockets fail, the system falls back to REST polling via the MarketDataService for price data (FR16)
   411‚Üí**And** during REST-only mode, `statusStore` reflects `'stale'` if no update received within 30 seconds
   412‚Üí**And** the dashboard remains functional displaying last known data when all external APIs are unreachable (NFR15)
   413‚Üí**And** when a higher-priority source becomes available again, the ConnectionManager reconnects to it
   414‚Üí**And** `connection-manager.test.ts` covers the full fallback chain: Binance ‚Üí CoinCap ‚Üí REST
   415‚Üí
   416‚Üí### Story 4.3: Status Indicator & Stale Data Badge
   417‚Üí
   418‚ÜíAs a user,
   419‚ÜíI want to see a subtle connection status indicator and know when displayed data may be outdated,
   420‚ÜíSo that I can trust the data when it's live and be aware when it's stale.
   421‚Üí
   422‚Üí**Acceptance Criteria:**
   423‚Üí
   424‚Üí**Given** the Status Indicator island is hydrated and subscribes to statusStore
   425‚Üí**When** statusStore changes value
   426‚Üí**Then** the indicator displays at the bottom center of the viewport as a 6px dot + text at 11px `text-muted` (UX-DR7)
   427‚Üí**And** Live state shows: green dot (`status-live`) + "Live" text ‚Äî barely visible, quiet good state
   428‚Üí**And** Reconnecting state shows: amber dot (`status-reconnecting`) + "Reconnecting..." text with dot pulsing animation (1.5s ease-in-out cycle, opacity 0.4‚Äì1.0) (FR14)
   429‚Üí**And** Stale state shows: red dot (`status-stale`) + "Connection lost" text, static (no animation)
   430‚Üí**And** Fallback state shows: green dot (`status-live`) + "Live ‚Äî CoinCap" text
   431‚Üí**And** state transitions fade over 300ms ease-in-out ‚Äî no abrupt switches
   432‚Üí**And** when statusStore is `'stale'` (no price update for >30 seconds), a "Stale" badge appears on the Price Card in `status-stale` color (FR15)
   433‚Üí**And** the stale badge does not hide or replace the price value ‚Äî it's an addition
   434‚Üí**And** when connection restores and a new price tick arrives, the stale badge fades out over 300ms
   435‚Üí**And** all animations respect `prefers-reduced-motion` (instant transitions, no pulsing)
   436‚Üí**And** the Status Indicator uses `<div role="status" aria-live="polite">` so screen readers announce state changes
   437‚Üí

    18‚Üí# Architecture Decision Document
    19‚Üí
    20‚Üí_This document builds collaboratively through step-by-step discovery. Sections are appended as we work through each architectural decision together._
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí## Project Context Analysis
    23‚Üí
    24‚Üí### Requirements Overview
    25‚Üí
    26‚Üí**Functional Requirements:**
    27‚Üí23 FRs across 6 categories. The dominant category is Connection Resilience (FR11‚ÄìFR16) ‚Äî 6 requirements governing WebSocket lifecycle, multi-source fallback, and stale data handling. This is the most architecturally significant area despite the project's low overall complexity. The remaining FRs cover straightforward data display (price, change, market cap, chart) and visual presentation (dark theme, animations, responsive layout).
    28‚Üí
    29‚Üí**Non-Functional Requirements:**
    30‚Üí17 NFRs with specific quantified targets. Performance NFRs (1‚Äì8) define a tight bundle budget (<100KB JS, <50KB chart lib) and strict rendering metrics (CLS = 0, LCP <1.5s, 60 FPS). Integration NFRs (9‚Äì14) specify WebSocket connection behavior (500ms connect, exponential backoff 1s‚Äì30s, 60s REST polling). Reliability NFRs (15‚Äì17) require graceful degradation and no memory leaks over 24h+ sessions.
    31‚Üí
    32‚Üí**Scale & Complexity:**
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üí- Primary domain: Frontend web ‚Äî real-time data visualization
    35‚Üí- Complexity level: Low
    36‚Üí- Estimated architectural components: 5 UI components + 1 WebSocket service layer + 1 REST polling service + 1 shared theme constants module
    37‚Üí
    38‚Üí### Technical Constraints & Dependencies
    39‚Üí
    40‚Üí- **Framework locked:** Astro JS with Islands Architecture ‚Äî non-negotiable
    41‚Üí- **Styling locked:** Tailwind CSS with custom semantic tokens
    42‚Üí- **Chart library:** lightweight-charts (TradingView) ‚Äî chosen for finance-native API, ~40KB gzipped
    43‚Üí- **External API dependencies (no auth required):**
    44‚Üí  - Binance WebSocket (primary real-time) ‚Äî 24h connection expiry, potential geo-restrictions
    45‚Üí  - CoinCap WebSocket (fallback real-time) ‚Äî simpler, universal access
    46‚Üí  - CoinGecko REST (supplementary data) ‚Äî 30 req/min free tier, 60s polling interval
    47‚Üí- **No backend, no SSR, no routing, no SEO, no user data storage**
    48‚Üí- **Browser targets:** Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge (latest 2 versions) ‚Äî backdrop-filter supported across all
    49‚Üí
    50‚Üí### Cross-Cutting Concerns Identified
    51‚Üí
    52‚Üí1. **WebSocket connection state** ‚Äî Shared across Price Card, Status Indicator, and fallback logic. Needs a unified connection manager that multiple islands can observe.
    53‚Üí2. **Design token consistency** ‚Äî Tailwind config tokens must stay in sync with lightweight-charts JS API colors. Solved via shared `theme-constants.ts` file.
    54‚Üí3. **Stale data handling** ‚Äî Affects Price Card (badge display), Status Indicator (state text), and tab title (should it show stale price?). Needs a consistent staleness threshold (>30s).
    55‚Üí4. **Animation/motion standards** ‚Äî Consistent timing (300ms/400ms/500ms), easing curves, and `prefers-reduced-motion` support across all components.
    56‚Üí5. **Number formatting** ‚Äî USD formatting with comma separators, abbreviated market cap, consistent decimal places across all data displays.
    57‚Üí
    58‚Üí## Starter Template Evaluation
    59‚Üí
    60‚Üí### Primary Technology Domain
    61‚Üí
    62‚ÜíFrontend web ‚Äî single-page real-time dashboard built on Astro Islands Architecture.
    63‚Üí
    64‚Üí### Starter Options Considered
    65‚Üí
    66‚Üí| Option | Template | Fit | Verdict |
    67‚Üí|---|---|---|---|
    68‚Üí| A | `create astro -- --template minimal` | Perfect ‚Äî clean slate, zero cruft | **Selected** |
    69‚Üí| B | Astroplate / AstroWind / Astroship | Poor ‚Äî blog/content-site scaffolding to remove | Rejected |
    70‚Üí| C | Manual setup | No advantage over CLI wizard | Rejected |
    71‚Üí
    72‚Üí### Selected Starter: Astro Minimal + CLI Integrations
    73‚Üí
    74‚Üí**Rationale:** The project has exactly 1 page, 5 components, and no content management. Any opinionated starter adds complexity to remove. The minimal template + `astro add` commands give us the exact stack specified in the PRD with zero waste.
    75‚Üí
    76‚Üí**Initialization Commands:**
    77‚Üí
    78‚Üí```bash
    79‚Üínpm create astro@latest -- --template minimal --typescript strict
    80‚Üínpx astro add tailwind
    81‚Üínpm install lightweight-charts @fontsource/jetbrains-mono @fontsource/inter
    82‚Üínpm install -D eslint prettier eslint-plugin-astro prettier-plugin-astro
    83‚Üínpm install -D vitest
    84‚Üí```
    85‚Üí
    86‚Üí**Package manager:** npm (explicit choice ‚Äî simplest for solo developer, no pnpm/yarn complexity needed).
    87‚Üí
    88‚Üí### Architectural Decisions Provided by Starter
    89‚Üí
    90‚Üí**Language & Runtime:**
    91‚Üí- Astro 6.x (current stable 6.0.8) ‚Äî requires Node 22+
    92‚Üí- TypeScript in strict mode ‚Äî catches type errors early, appropriate for intermediate developer
    93‚Üí- Astro's built-in TypeScript support (no separate tsconfig needed beyond Astro defaults)
    94‚Üí- Note: Astro 6 is a recent stable release. Headline features (redesigned dev server, CSP APIs, live content collections) aren't needed for this project ‚Äî we're on Astro 6 because `create astro@latest` gives us Astro 6. Acceptable early-adopter risk for a personal project with no user data.
    95‚Üí
    96‚Üí**Styling Solution:**
    97‚Üí- Tailwind CSS v4 via `@tailwindcss/vite` plugin
    98‚Üí- CSS-first configuration: `@theme` directives in `src/styles/global.css` replace `tailwind.config.js` for token definition
    99‚Üí- Design tokens (`accent-primary`, `bg-base`, `text-muted`, etc.) defined as CSS custom properties via `@theme`
   100‚Üí- **Tailwind v4 ‚Üî lightweight-charts token strategy:** Colors needed by the lightweight-charts JS API cannot be read from CSS `@theme` at import time. For v1, duplicate color values in a `theme-constants.ts` file ‚Äî acceptable sync risk for a solo developer with 5 color values. Refactor to runtime `getComputedStyle()` reads if a theme switcher is ever added.
   101‚Üí
   102‚Üí**Build Tooling:**
   103‚Üí- Vite (bundled with Astro) ‚Äî HMR, tree-shaking, optimized production builds
   104‚Üí- Astro's static output mode (`output: 'static'`) ‚Äî generates pure HTML/CSS/JS files
   105‚Üí- Automatic CSS purging via Tailwind v4
   106‚Üí
   107‚Üí**Code Quality:**
   108‚Üí- ESLint with `eslint-plugin-astro` for Astro-aware linting
   109‚Üí- Prettier with `prettier-plugin-astro` for consistent formatting
   110‚Üí- Configured from first commit ‚Äî important if project becomes open-source in Phase 3
   111‚Üí
   112‚Üí**Testing Framework:**
   113‚Üí- Vitest included in dev dependencies from project init
   114‚Üí- Recommended test strategy for v1: unit tests for WebSocket manager and number formatting utilities, single Playwright smoke test for "page loads, price appears"
   115‚Üí- Note: if chart config uses runtime `getComputedStyle()` in future, CSS values won't be available in Node.js test environment ‚Äî the duplicated `theme-constants.ts` approach keeps test imports trivial
   116‚Üí
   117‚Üí**Code Organization:**
   118‚Üí- `src/pages/` ‚Äî single `index.astro` page
   119‚Üí- `src/components/` ‚Äî Astro components (islands)
   120‚Üí- `src/layouts/` ‚Äî page layout wrapper
   121‚Üí- `src/styles/` ‚Äî global CSS with Tailwind `@import` and `@theme` tokens
   122‚Üí- `src/lib/` ‚Äî shared utilities (`theme-constants.ts`, formatting helpers, WebSocket manager)
   123‚Üí
   124‚Üí**Development Experience:**
   125‚Üí- Astro dev server with HMR
   126‚Üí- TypeScript type checking
   127‚Üí- Tailwind CSS IntelliSense (VS Code extension)
   128‚Üí
   129‚Üí**Note:** Project initialization using these commands should be the first implementation story.
   130‚Üí
   131‚Üí## Core Architectural Decisions
   132‚Üí
   133‚Üí### Decision Priority Analysis
   134‚Üí
   135‚Üí**Critical Decisions (Block Implementation):**
   136‚Üí1. Inter-island state management ‚Äî Nano Stores
   137‚Üí2. Island UI framework ‚Äî Vanilla TypeScript (no framework)
   138‚Üí3. WebSocket manager architecture ‚Äî single manager, multiple consumers via stores
   139‚Üí
   140‚Üí**Important Decisions (Shape Architecture):**
   141‚Üí4. Message normalization ‚Äî common `PriceTick` interface across data sources
   142‚Üí5. Stale data threshold ‚Äî 30 seconds
   143‚Üí
   144‚Üí**Deferred Decisions (Post-MVP):**
   145‚Üí- Hosting provider selection (Phase 2)
   146‚Üí- CI/CD pipeline (Phase 2)
   147‚Üí- PWA configuration (Phase 2)
   148‚Üí- Component library extraction (Phase 3)
   149‚Üí
   150‚Üí### Data Architecture
   151‚Üí
   152‚ÜíNo database. No persistent storage. All data is ephemeral ‚Äî sourced from external APIs and held in memory via Nano Stores.
   153‚Üí
   154‚Üí**Data flow:**
   155‚Üí```
   156‚ÜíBinance WebSocket ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí ConnectionManager ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Nano Store (priceStore) ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Price Card island
   157‚ÜíCoinCap WebSocket ‚îÄ‚îò  (fallback)           Nano Store (statusStore) ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Status Indicator island
   158‚Üí                                            Nano Store (priceStore) ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Tab title updater
   159‚Üí
   160‚ÜíCoinGecko REST ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí MarketDataService ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Nano Store (marketStore) ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Market Cap Card island
   161‚Üí                     (60s polling)         Nano Store (chartStore)  ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Chart Card island
   162‚Üí```
   163‚Üí
   164‚Üí**Data interfaces:**
   165‚Üí- `PriceTick`: `{ price: number, timestamp: number, direction: 'up' | 'down' | 'neutral' }` ‚Äî normalized from both Binance and CoinCap formats
   166‚Üí- `MarketData`: `{ marketCap: number, change24h: number, changePercent24h: number, high24h: number, low24h: number }` ‚Äî from CoinGecko REST
   167‚Üí- `ChartPoint`: `{ time: number, value: number }` ‚Äî hourly price points for lightweight-charts
   168‚Üí- `ConnectionStatus`: `'connecting' | 'live' | 'reconnecting' | 'stale' | 'fallback'`
   169‚Üí
   170‚Üí### Authentication & Security
   171‚Üí
   172‚ÜíNot applicable. No user accounts, no API keys, no sensitive data. All external APIs are public and keyless.
   173‚Üí
   174‚Üí**Client-side security considerations:**
   175‚Üí- No user input to sanitize (read-only dashboard)
   176‚Üí- WebSocket connections use `wss://` (TLS encrypted)
   177‚Üí- No localStorage/sessionStorage usage ‚Äî no data persistence to protect
   178‚Üí- Content Security Policy: consider adding CSP headers when deploying (Phase 2) to restrict WebSocket origins
   179‚Üí
   180‚Üí### API & Communication Patterns
   181‚Üí
   182‚Üí**WebSocket Manager (`src/lib/connection-manager.ts`):**
   183‚Üí- Singleton pattern ‚Äî one instance manages the entire WebSocket lifecycle
   184‚Üí- Fallback chain: Binance `wss://stream.binance.com:9443/ws/btcusdt@trade` ‚Üí CoinCap `wss://ws.coincap.io/prices?assets=bitcoin`
   185‚Üí- Reconnection: exponential backoff starting at 1s, doubling to max 30s cap
   186‚Üí- 24h expiry: Binance hard-expires connections at 24 hours ‚Äî handled as a standard reconnect cycle
   187‚Üí- Stale threshold: 30 seconds without a price update ‚Üí `ConnectionStatus` transitions to `'stale'`
   188‚Üí- Publishes normalized `PriceTick` to `priceStore` (Nano Store)
   189‚Üí- Publishes `ConnectionStatus` to `statusStore` (Nano Store)
   190‚Üí
   191‚Üí**REST Polling Service (`src/lib/market-data-service.ts`):**
   192‚Üí- Polls CoinGecko REST API every 60 seconds (within 30 req/min free tier)
   193‚Üí- Fetches: market cap, 24h change, 24h high/low, hourly historical data for chart
   194‚Üí- On API failure: retains last known data, does not clear stores
   195‚Üí- Publishes to `marketStore` and `chartStore` (Nano Stores)
   196‚Üí
   197‚Üí**Error Handling Standards:**
   198‚Üí- WebSocket errors ‚Üí silent reconnection via backoff, status indicator updates
   199‚Üí- REST errors ‚Üí retain stale data, log to console, no user-facing error UI
   200‚Üí- No thrown exceptions reaching the UI ‚Äî all error states expressed via store values
   201‚Üí- Console logging for debugging only ‚Äî no external error reporting service in v1
   202‚Üí
   203‚Üí### Frontend Architecture
   204‚Üí
   205‚Üí**State Management: Nano Stores**
   206‚Üí- Decision: Nano Stores (~1KB) as the reactive state layer shared across Astro islands
   207‚Üí- Rationale: Astro's officially recommended approach for inter-island communication. Reactive, tiny, framework-agnostic.
   208‚Üí- Stores:
   209‚Üí  - `priceStore` ‚Äî current `PriceTick` (written by ConnectionManager)
   210‚Üí  - `statusStore` ‚Äî current `ConnectionStatus` (written by ConnectionManager)
   211‚Üí  - `marketStore` ‚Äî current `MarketData` (written by MarketDataService)
   212‚Üí  - `chartStore` ‚Äî array of `ChartPoint` (written by MarketDataService)
   213‚Üí
   214‚Üí**Island UI Framework: Vanilla TypeScript**
   215‚Üí- Decision: No UI framework. Islands use Astro `<script>` tags with `client:load` for direct DOM updates.
   216‚Üí- Rationale: Islands perform simple operations (update text, toggle classes, call lightweight-charts API). Framework overhead adds bundle weight without proportional value. Keeps total JS well within <100KB budget.
   217‚Üí
   218‚Üí**Component Architecture:**
   219‚Üí- 5 Astro components as defined in UX spec: Page Shell (static), Price Card (island), Market Cap Card (island), Chart Card (island), Status Indicator (island)
   220‚Üí- Each island subscribes to relevant Nano Stores and updates DOM directly
   221‚Üí- No component nesting beyond page ‚Üí island ‚Äî flat hierarchy
   222‚Üí
   223‚Üí**Bundle Optimization:**
   224‚Üí- Astro static output ‚Äî only island JS ships to client
   225‚Üí- Nano Stores: ~1KB gzipped
   226‚Üí- lightweight-charts: ~40KB gzipped
   227‚Üí- @fontsource fonts: self-hosted, subset to Latin characters
   228‚Üí- Estimated total hydrated JS: ~50-60KB gzipped (well within 100KB budget)
   229‚Üí
   230‚Üí### Infrastructure & Deployment
   231‚Üí
   232‚Üí**v1: Local Development Only**
   233‚Üí- `npm run dev` ‚Äî Astro dev server with HMR
   234‚Üí- No deployment pipeline, no hosting configuration
   235‚Üí- Environment configuration: API endpoints as hardcoded constants in `src/lib/` (all public, no secrets)
   236‚Üí
   237‚Üí**Phase 2 Deployment Path (documented, not implemented):**
   238‚Üí- Target: Vercel or Netlify (static hosting)
   239‚Üí- Build: `npm run build` ‚Üí `dist/` directory with static HTML/CSS/JS
   240‚Üí- No server-side requirements ‚Äî pure static deployment
   241‚Üí- Consider moving API endpoints to `.env` if multiple environments needed
   242‚Üí
   243‚Üí### Decision Impact Analysis
   244‚Üí
   245‚Üí**Implementation Sequence:**
   246‚Üí1. Project initialization (starter commands)
   247‚Üí2. Tailwind `@theme` token configuration + `theme-constants.ts`
   248‚Üí3. Nano Stores setup (4 stores with TypeScript interfaces)
   249‚Üí4. ConnectionManager (WebSocket lifecycle + fallback chain)
   250‚Üí5. MarketDataService (REST polling)
   251‚Üí6. Page Shell (static layout grid)
   252‚Üí7. Price Card island (subscribes to priceStore + statusStore)
   253‚Üí8. Status Indicator island (subscribes to statusStore)
   254‚Üí9. Market Cap Card island (subscribes to marketStore)
   255‚Üí10. Chart Card island (subscribes to chartStore, configures lightweight-charts)
   256‚Üí
   257‚Üí**Cross-Component Dependencies:**
   258‚Üí- ConnectionManager ‚Üí priceStore, statusStore (writes)
   259‚Üí- MarketDataService ‚Üí marketStore, chartStore (writes)
   260‚Üí- Price Card ‚Üí priceStore, statusStore (reads)
   261‚Üí- Market Cap Card ‚Üí marketStore (reads)
   262‚Üí- Chart Card ‚Üí chartStore (reads)
   263‚Üí- Status Indicator ‚Üí statusStore (reads)
   264‚Üí- theme-constants.ts ‚Üí Chart Card (color configuration)
   265‚Üí
   266‚Üí## Implementation Patterns & Consistency Rules
   267‚Üí
   268‚Üí### Critical Conflict Points Identified
   269‚Üí
   270‚Üí12 areas where AI agents could make different choices, organized into 5 categories. All patterns below are mandatory for implementation consistency.
   271‚Üí
   272‚Üí### Naming Patterns
   273‚Üí
   274‚Üí**File Naming:**
   275‚Üí- Astro components: PascalCase ‚Äî `PriceCard.astro`, `ChartCard.astro`, `StatusIndicator.astro`, `PageShell.astro`
   276‚Üí- TypeScript modules: kebab-case ‚Äî `connection-manager.ts`, `market-data-service.ts`, `theme-constants.ts`
   277‚Üí- Store files: kebab-case ‚Äî `price-store.ts`, `status-store.ts`, `market-store.ts`, `chart-store.ts`
   278‚Üí- Test files: co-located with source, `.test.ts` suffix ‚Äî `connection-manager.test.ts`, `format-utils.test.ts`
   279‚Üí- CSS: single global file ‚Äî `src/styles/global.css`
   280‚Üí
   281‚Üí**Code Naming:**
   282‚Üí- Variables and functions: camelCase ‚Äî `priceStore`, `formatCurrency()`, `handlePriceTick()`
   283‚Üí- Types and interfaces: PascalCase ‚Äî `PriceTick`, `MarketData`, `ConnectionStatus`
   284‚Üí- Constants: UPPER_SNAKE_CASE ‚Äî `STALE_THRESHOLD_MS`, `WS_BINANCE_URL`, `REST_POLL_INTERVAL_MS`
   285‚Üí- Store names: camelCase with `Store` suffix ‚Äî `priceStore`, `statusStore`, `marketStore`, `chartStore`
   286‚Üí- Event handlers: `handle` + noun + verb pattern ‚Äî `handlePriceUpdate()`, `handleConnectionDrop()`
   287‚Üí
   288‚Üí**CSS/Tailwind Naming:**
   289‚Üí- Semantic token names in `@theme`: kebab-case with category prefix ‚Äî `--color-accent-primary`, `--color-bg-base`, `--color-text-muted`
   290‚Üí- `@apply` compositions (max 3): `.card-glass`, `.price-text`, `.status-badge`
   291‚Üí- Astro scoped style keyframe names: kebab-case ‚Äî `@keyframes price-flash`, `@keyframes fade-in`
   292‚Üí
   293‚Üí### Structure Patterns
   294‚Üí
   295‚Üí**Project Organization:**
   296‚Üí```
   297‚Üísrc/
   298‚Üí‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ pages/
   299‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ index.astro              # Single page entry
   300‚Üí‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ layouts/
   301‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ Layout.astro             # Page shell wrapper
   302‚Üí‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ components/
   303‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ PriceCard.astro          # Hydrated island
   304‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ MarketCapCard.astro      # Hydrated island
   305‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ChartCard.astro          # Hydrated island
   306‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ StatusIndicator.astro    # Hydrated island
   307‚Üí‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ lib/
   308‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ stores/
   309‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ price-store.ts
   310‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ status-store.ts
   311‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ market-store.ts
   312‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ chart-store.ts
   313‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ services/
   314‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ connection-manager.ts
   315‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ market-data-service.ts
   316‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ utils/
   317‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ format-utils.ts      # Number formatting helpers
   318‚Üí‚îÇ   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ theme-constants.ts       # Shared color values for lightweight-charts
   319‚Üí‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ styles/
   320‚Üí    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ global.css               # Tailwind @import, @theme tokens, @apply compositions
   321‚Üí```
   322‚Üí
   323‚Üí**Rules:**
   324‚Üí- Components in `src/components/` ‚Äî flat, no subdirectories (only 4 components)
   325‚Üí- Business logic in `src/lib/services/` ‚Äî never in components
   326‚Üí- Stores in `src/lib/stores/` ‚Äî one file per store
   327‚Üí- Utility functions in `src/lib/utils/` ‚Äî pure functions only, no side effects
   328‚Üí- Tests co-located: `src/lib/services/connection-manager.test.ts` next to source file
   329‚Üí
   330‚Üí### Store Patterns
   331‚Üí
   332‚Üí**Nano Store conventions:**
   333‚Üí
   334‚Üí```typescript
   335‚Üí// Store definition pattern (every store file follows this)
   336‚Üíimport { atom } from 'nanostores';
   337‚Üíimport type { PriceTick } from '../types';
   338‚Üí
   339‚Üíexport const priceStore = atom<PriceTick | null>(null);
   340‚Üí```
   341‚Üí
   342‚Üí**Rules:**
   343‚Üí- All stores initialize to `null` (not undefined, not default objects)
   344‚Üí- Only services write to stores ‚Äî never components
   345‚Üí- Components subscribe in `<script>` blocks using `store.subscribe()`
   346‚Üí- One store per concern ‚Äî never combine unrelated data in a single store
   347‚Üí- Type every store with explicit generic: `atom<PriceTick | null>`
   348‚Üí
   349‚Üí### Format Patterns
   350‚Üí
   351‚Üí**Number Formatting (all handled by `format-utils.ts`):**
   352‚Üí- Price: `$68,432.17` ‚Äî USD, comma separators, always 2 decimal places
   353‚Üí- Percentage: `+2.3%` or `-1.8%` ‚Äî sign always shown, 1 decimal place
   354‚Üí- Absolute change: `+$1,534.22` or `-$892.40` ‚Äî sign + USD, 2 decimal places
   355‚Üí- Market cap: `$1.34T` or `$892.45B` ‚Äî abbreviated with 2 decimal places
   356‚Üí- 24h range: `$66.9k ‚Äî $68.9k` ‚Äî abbreviated with 1 decimal place
   357‚Üí- Chart tooltip: `$68,432.17 at 14:30` ‚Äî full price + 24h time (no date, no seconds)
   358‚Üí
   359‚Üí**Rules:**
   360‚Üí- All formatting goes through `format-utils.ts` helper functions ‚Äî never inline formatting in components
   361‚Üí- Use `Intl.NumberFormat` as the base ‚Äî no manual string building
   362‚Üí- Direction indicators always pair symbol with color: `‚Üë` green, `‚Üì` red, `‚Üí` white (neutral)
   363‚Üí- Never use color alone to communicate direction
   364‚Üí
   365‚Üí### Communication Patterns
   366‚Üí
   367‚Üí**Store update flow (unidirectional):**
   368‚Üí```
   369‚ÜíExternal API ‚Üí Service ‚Üí Store ‚Üí Component ‚Üí DOM
   370‚Üí```
   371‚Üí
   372‚Üí**Rules:**
   373‚Üí- Services are the only writers to stores
   374‚Üí- Components are read-only subscribers ‚Äî never call `store.set()` from a component
   375‚Üí- No component-to-component communication ‚Äî always go through stores
   376‚Üí- No direct DOM manipulation outside of store subscription callbacks in island `<script>` blocks
   377‚Üí
   378‚Üí**WebSocket message handling:**
   379‚Üí- Raw messages are parsed and normalized in `connection-manager.ts` ‚Äî components never see raw WebSocket data
   380‚Üí- Normalization happens before store write ‚Äî stores only contain clean `PriceTick` objects
   381‚Üí- Binance format: `{ p: "68432.17", ... }` ‚Üí `{ price: 68432.17, timestamp: Date.now(), direction: 'up' }`
   382‚Üí- CoinCap format: `{ bitcoin: "68432.17" }` ‚Üí same normalized `PriceTick`
   383‚Üí
   384‚Üí### Process Patterns
   385‚Üí
   386‚Üí**Error Handling:**
   387‚Üí- WebSocket errors: catch silently, update `statusStore` to `'reconnecting'`, trigger backoff ‚Äî no console.error for expected disconnects
   388‚Üí- REST errors: catch silently, retain last store values, `console.warn()` with endpoint and status code
   389‚Üí- Parsing errors (malformed API response): `console.error()` with raw payload, skip the update, do not crash the island
   390‚Üí- Never throw from a store subscription callback ‚Äî wrap in try/catch
   391‚Üí- Never show error UI to the user ‚Äî the UX spec explicitly avoids error states. Use stale indicators instead.
   392‚Üí
   393‚Üí**Initialization sequence:**
   394‚Üí- Services initialize in `Layout.astro` via a single `<script>` block that imports and starts `ConnectionManager` and `MarketDataService`
   395‚Üí- Islands hydrate via `client:load` and immediately subscribe to relevant stores
   396‚Üí- Order doesn't matter ‚Äî stores start as `null`, components render empty state, data appears when stores update
   397‚Üí
   398‚Üí**Animation implementation:**
   399‚Üí- All durations as constants in a shared location (CSS custom properties or `theme-constants.ts`)
   400‚Üí- Price flash: CSS transition on `color` property, triggered by toggling a data attribute (`data-flash="up"`)
   401‚Üí- Entry fade-in: CSS transition on `opacity`, triggered by removing a `data-loading` attribute
   402‚Üí- Chart draw: lightweight-charts built-in animation API
   403‚Üí- Always check `prefers-reduced-motion` ‚Äî if enabled, set all durations to 0ms
   404‚Üí
   405‚Üí### Enforcement Guidelines
   406‚Üí
   407‚Üí**All AI Agents MUST:**
   408‚Üí1. Follow the file naming and project structure patterns exactly ‚Äî no alternative organizations
   409‚Üí2. Use `format-utils.ts` for all number display ‚Äî no inline `toFixed()` or manual formatting
   410‚Üí3. Write to stores only from services, read from stores only in components ‚Äî no exceptions
   411‚Üí4. Normalize all external API data before writing to stores ‚Äî components never parse raw responses
   412‚Üí5. Co-locate test files with source files using `.test.ts` suffix
   413‚Üí
   414‚Üí**Anti-Patterns (never do these):**
   415‚Üí- Creating a `utils/index.ts` barrel file ‚Äî import directly from specific modules
   416‚Üí- Adding a UI framework "just for one component" ‚Äî all islands use vanilla TS
   417‚Üí- Storing derived data in stores ‚Äî compute in components (e.g., don't store formatted strings)
   418‚Üí- Using `setInterval` for REST polling ‚Äî use recursive `setTimeout` to prevent drift on slow responses
   419‚Üí- Adding loading spinners or skeleton screens ‚Äî the empty card with labels IS the loading state
   420‚Üí

   487‚Üí### Architectural Boundaries

   541‚Üí### Integration Points
   542‚Üí
   543‚Üí**Internal Communication:**
   544‚ÜíAll inter-component communication flows through Nano Stores. No direct imports between components. No DOM events for data passing. The store is the only integration point.

   546‚Üí**External Integrations:**
   547‚Üí
   548‚Üí| Integration | Endpoint | Rate | Error Strategy |
   549‚Üí|---|---|---|---|

   551‚Üí| CoinCap WS | `wss://ws.coincap.io/prices?assets=bitcoin` | Continuous | Fallback to REST polling |
   552‚Üí| CoinGecko REST | `https://api.coingecko.com/api/v3/...` | Every 60s | Retain last known data |

   555‚Üí```

   560‚Üí
   561‚Üí[CoinGecko REST] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí market-data-service.ts ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí marketStore ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí MarketCapCard (DOM update)
   562‚Üí                                             ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí chartStore  ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí ChartCard (chart update)

   623‚Üí### Gap Analysis Results
   624‚Üí
   625‚Üí**Gap Found: NFR17 ‚Äî Memory Leak Prevention (24h+ Sessions)**
   626‚Üí
   627‚ÜíPriority: Important ‚Äî addressed below.
   628‚Üí
   629‚ÜíThe architecture did not explicitly specify memory management for long-running sessions. Three leak vectors identified and resolved:
   630‚Üí
   631‚Üí**1. WebSocket listener accumulation:**
   632‚Üí- Risk: Each reconnect cycle could add new `onmessage`/`onerror` listeners without removing old ones
   633‚Üí- Rule: ConnectionManager MUST call `ws.close()` and null the reference before creating a new WebSocket instance. Never add listeners to a WebSocket that's being replaced.
   634‚Üí
   635‚Üí**2. Chart data array growth:**
   636‚Üí- Risk: `chartStore` array grows unbounded as new hourly data points arrive
   637‚Üí- Rule: Cap `chartStore` at 24 entries (24 hours of hourly data). When appending a new point, shift the oldest. Never let the array exceed the 24h window.
   638‚Üí
   639‚Üí**3. Timer reference leaks:**
   640‚Üí- Risk: `setTimeout` references from REST polling or reconnection backoff could accumulate if not properly cleared
   641‚Üí- Rule: Store all timer IDs. Clear previous timer before scheduling a new one. Use a single `AbortController` per service for coordinated cleanup if the page is ever torn down.
   642‚Üí
   643‚Üí**No other gaps found.** All critical, important, and nice-to-have areas are covered.

Based on the search results, here's information about the lightweight-charts v5 `createChart` and area series TypeScript API:

## Creating a Chart

The `createChart` function returns an `IChartApi` object that allows you to interact with the created chart. You can use it like this:

```typescript
import { createChart } from 'lightweight-charts';
const chart = createChart(container);
```

## Adding an Area Series

The package includes TypeScript declarations, enabling seamless integration within TypeScript projects. To add an area series in v5, use the unified series creation API with a single `addSeries` function, where individual series types must now be imported separately for ES modules:

```typescript
import { createChart, AreaSeries } from 'lightweight-charts';
const chart = createChart(container);
const areaSeries = chart.addSeries(AreaSeries, {
  lineColor: '#2962FF',
  topColor: '#2962FF',
  bottomColor: 'rgba(41, 98, 255, 0.28)'
});
```

## Setting Data

When the series is created, you can populate it with data, and the API calls remain the same regardless of the series type, although the data format may vary. For area series, the data format requires `time` and `value` fields.

The current version is v5.1, and comprehensive documentation is available at the TradingView Lightweight Charts official documentation site.

Based on the search results, I found detailed information about the CoinGecko API `/coins/markets` endpoint with the parameters you specified.

## CoinGecko API `/coins/markets` Response

The `/coins/markets` endpoint allows you to query market data by specifying the currency to compare to (vs_currency ‚Äì typically 'usd') and the IDs of specific coins.

### Example Response JSON

A response from this endpoint with Bitcoin includes fields such as: id, symbol, name, image, current_price, market_cap, market_cap_rank, fully_diluted_valuation, total_volume, high_24h, low_24h, price_change_24h, price_change_percentage_24h, market_cap_change_24h, market_cap_change_percentage_24h, circulating_supply, total_supply, max_supply, ath, ath_change_percentage, ath_date, atl, atl_change_percentage, atl_date, roi, and last_updated.

The fields you specifically asked about are all included in the response:
- **high_24h**: The highest price in the last 24 hours
- **low_24h**: The lowest price in the last 24 hours  
- **price_change_24h**: The price change amount over the last 24 hours
- **market_cap**: The total market capitalization

The endpoint accepts the `ids` parameter to filter for specific coins (comma-separated if querying more than one coin).

I found comprehensive information about using `subscribeCrosshairMove` with tooltips in lightweight-charts v5. Here are the key examples and information:

## Basic subscribeCrosshairMove Usage

A basic example shows defining a handler function that logs the crosshair position: `function myCrosshairMoveHandler(param) { if (!param.point) { return; } console.log('Crosshair moved to ${param.point.x}, ${param.point.y}. The time is ${param.time}.'); } chart.subscribeCrosshairMove(myCrosshairMoveHandler);`

## Tooltip Implementation

To add a tooltip to the chart, you can subscribe to crosshair move events and manually update the content within an HTML tooltip element and change its position.

A common implementation checks if the point is valid, retrieves series data, and positions the tooltip: `chart.subscribeCrosshairMove(param => { if ( param.point === undefined || !param.time || param.point.x < 0 || param.point.y < 0 ) { toolTip.style.display = 'none'; } else { ... toolTip.innerHTML = '...'; toolTip.style.left = param.point.x + 'px'; toolTip.style.top = param.point.y + 'px'; } });`

## Getting Price Data

You can retrieve price data using `param.seriesData.get(series)` and check for value or close price properties to extract the price information for the tooltip.

The official Lightweight Charts documentation provides complete working examples with full code at https://tradingview.github.io/lightweight-charts/tutorials/how_to/tooltips

     4‚Üí  - prd.md

     6‚Üí  - product-brief-metrics-distillate.md
     7‚Üí---
     8‚Üí
     9‚Üí# UX Design Specification - Metrics
    10‚Üí
    11‚Üí**Author:** Dmytro
    12‚Üí**Date:** 2026-03-25
    13‚Üí
    14‚Üí---
    15‚Üí
    16‚Üí## Executive Summary
    17‚Üí
    18‚Üí### Project Vision
    19‚Üí
    20‚ÜíSingle-page, real-time Bitcoin dashboard designed around three UX pillars: speed (information available in < 1 second of glancing), aesthetics (dark crypto visual identity that feels premium and alive), and focus (one asset, one page, zero cognitive overhead). The product competes on depth of simplicity ‚Äî every design decision serves the "open tab, know the price, move on" experience.
    21‚Üí
    22‚Üí### Target Users
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíPrimary user: Dmytro, a developer who uses Metrics as a pinned browser tab for daily BTC price checking. Desktop-first with occasional mobile glances. Expects zero configuration, zero onboarding, and instant information delivery. Tech-savvy but values beauty and polish ‚Äî the dashboard should feel crafted, not cobbled.
    25‚Üí
    26‚Üí### Key Design Challenges
    27‚Üí
    28‚Üí- **Information hierarchy:** Four data elements (price, 24h change, market cap, chart) on a single screen require clear visual priority. Live price dominates; supporting data is secondary but instantly parseable.
    29‚Üí- **Dark theme readability:** Near-black backgrounds (#0a0a0f) with neon accents demand careful contrast management. Price figures must be high-contrast without causing eye strain during extended pinned-tab viewing.
    30‚Üí- **State transitions:** WebSocket connection states (connecting, live, reconnecting, stale) must be communicated subtly without disrupting the primary price display. No layout shift during any state change.
    31‚Üí
    32‚Üí### Design Opportunities
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üí- **Sub-second glanceability:** Nail the information hierarchy so the price registers in peripheral vision. Tab title with live price extends this to no-focus-needed awareness.
    35‚Üí- **Motion as polish:** Entry animations, price tick effects, and chart drawing create a sense of liveness and premium feel. Motion communicates "this is real-time" without needing a label.
    36‚Üí- **Dark crypto aesthetic as identity:** Glassmorphism cards, neon accents, and monospaced price typography create a distinctive visual brand that's portfolio-worthy and instantly recognizable.
    37‚Üí
    38‚Üí## Core User Experience
    39‚Üí
    40‚Üí### Defining Experience
    41‚Üí
    42‚ÜíThe core interaction is **the glance** ‚Äî a sub-second visual scan that delivers complete BTC awareness. No clicks, no scrolling, no reading. The price registers in peripheral vision from a pinned tab; supporting data (change, cap, trend) is absorbed in a single focused look. The product's entire value is delivered passively. The user invests zero effort and receives complete information.
    43‚Üí
    44‚Üí### Platform Strategy
    45‚Üí
    46‚Üí- **Primary platform:** Desktop web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge) as a pinned tab
    47‚Üí- **Secondary platform:** Mobile web browser for on-the-go checks
    48‚Üí- **Input model:** Read-only ‚Äî no mouse interaction required for core experience. Hover tooltips on chart are the only interactive element.
    49‚Üí- **Offline consideration:** Not applicable ‚Äî the product's value is real-time data. When offline, display last known data with stale indicator.
    50‚Üí- **Tab title:** Leveraged as a zero-focus information channel ‚Äî price visible without switching to the tab
    51‚Üí
    52‚Üí### Effortless Interactions
    53‚Üí
    54‚ÜíEverything that should happen without user intervention:
    55‚Üí
    56‚Üí- **Price updates:** WebSocket stream delivers ticks automatically ‚Äî no refresh, no polling button
    57‚Üí- **Data refresh:** Market cap and 24h stats refresh via REST every 60s ‚Äî invisible to user
    58‚Üí- **Reconnection:** WebSocket drops are handled silently with exponential backoff. User sees a subtle indicator only if reconnection takes more than a few seconds.
    59‚Üí- **Theme rendering:** Dark theme is the default and only theme ‚Äî renders immediately with no flash of white
    60‚Üí- **Initial hydration:** Static shell appears instantly; data fades in smoothly as WebSocket connects. No loading spinners, no skeleton screens.
    61‚Üí- **Responsive adaptation:** Layout shifts from horizontal (desktop) to stacked (mobile) based on viewport ‚Äî no user toggle
    62‚Üí
    63‚Üí### Critical Success Moments
    64‚Üí
    65‚Üí1. **The First Load (make-or-break):** Dark background fills the screen. Static shell renders. Price fades in with animation. Chart draws itself. Under 1.5 seconds from URL to live data. If this moment feels slow, broken, or visually jarring ‚Äî the product fails its promise.
    66‚Üí2. **The Daily Glance (retention):** User switches to pinned tab and price is already there, already current, already beautiful. This moment must be 100% reliable. Any stale data, broken layout, or disconnection erodes trust.
    67‚Üí3. **The Reconnect (resilience):** WiFi drops, WebSocket disconnects. The transition from "live" to "reconnecting" to "live again" must be smooth and nearly invisible. The user should barely notice it happened.
    68‚Üí
    69‚Üí### Experience Principles
    70‚Üí
    71‚Üí1. **Passive over active:** The user should never have to do anything. Information is delivered, not retrieved.
    72‚Üí2. **Instant over progressive:** Show the answer immediately. No loading states, no progressive disclosure, no "please wait." Static shell + data fade-in is the only acceptable load pattern.
    73‚Üí3. **Subtle over loud:** State changes (reconnecting, stale data) are communicated through understated visual cues ‚Äî never modals, toasts, or attention-grabbing alerts. The price display is always the loudest element.
    74‚Üí4. **Alive over static:** Gentle motion (price ticks, chart animation, fade-ins) communicates that the data is live and the system is working. Stillness should feel intentional, not broken.
    75‚Üí
    76‚Üí## Desired Emotional Response
    77‚Üí
    78‚Üí### Primary Emotional Goals
    79‚Üí
    80‚Üí- **Calm confidence:** "I know the price." Zero anxiety, zero effort. The information is just there, reliable and current. The feeling of a tool that works so well it disappears.
    81‚Üí- **Pride of ownership:** "I built this, it's beautiful, it's mine." The dashboard isn't just functional ‚Äî it's a personal artifact. The visual polish reflects the care that went into building it. Every glance reinforces "I made something good."
    82‚Üí- **Quiet control:** The sense of having exactly the information you need, presented exactly how you want it. No one else's ads, no one else's layout decisions, no one else's priorities. This is your window into BTC.
    83‚Üí
    84‚Üí### Emotional Journey Mapping
    85‚Üí
    86‚Üí| Moment | Desired Emotion | Design Lever |
    87‚Üí|---|---|---|
    88‚Üí| First load | Delight + pride ‚Äî "This looks incredible" | Dark theme fills screen, data animates in smoothly, immediate visual impact |
    89‚Üí| Daily glance | Calm confidence ‚Äî "I know" | Price is instantly visible, data is current, no surprises |
    90‚Üí| Price moving fast | Engaged awareness ‚Äî "I can see it happening" | Live ticking price creates a sense of connection to the market |
    91‚Üí| WiFi drops | Unbothered trust ‚Äî "It'll handle it" | Subtle reconnecting indicator, no alarm, no disruption |
    92‚Üí| Showing someone | Pride ‚Äî "Look what I built" | The aesthetic sells itself. Glassmorphism, neon, monospace ‚Äî it looks impressive |
    93‚Üí| Mobile check | Same confidence, smaller screen ‚Äî "Still works perfectly" | Responsive layout preserves the feeling, not just the function |
    94‚Üí
    95‚Üí### Micro-Emotions
    96‚Üí
    97‚Üí**Cultivate:**
    98‚Üí- **Confidence** over confusion ‚Äî the data hierarchy is instantly clear, never ambiguous
    99‚Üí- **Trust** over skepticism ‚Äî live indicators and smooth reconnection prove the data is real and current
   100‚Üí- **Delight** over mere satisfaction ‚Äî the animations, typography, and visual details reward attention
   101‚Üí- **Ownership** over generic utility ‚Äî this feels personal, not like a commodity widget
   102‚Üí
   103‚Üí**Prevent:**
   104‚Üí- **Anxiety** ‚Äî never show alarming error states. Connection issues are handled quietly.
   105‚Üí- **Doubt** ‚Äî never leave the user wondering if the price is current. Stale data is explicitly marked.
   106‚Üí- **Boredom** ‚Äî the subtle motion (price ticks, chart updates) keeps the display feeling alive without being distracting
   107‚Üí
   108‚Üí### Design Implications
   109‚Üí
   110‚Üí- **Pride of craft ‚Üí Visual polish is non-negotiable.** Every pixel matters. Glassmorphism blur must be smooth, typography spacing must be precise, animations must be 60 FPS. This is a portfolio piece ‚Äî it should look like one.
   111‚Üí- **Calm confidence ‚Üí Information hierarchy is the #1 UX priority.** The price must be the loudest element by far. Supporting data is clearly secondary. No visual competition.
   112‚Üí- **Trust ‚Üí State communication must be honest but calm.** "Reconnecting..." in muted text ‚Äî never a red error banner. Stale data badge ‚Äî never a blocking modal. The system tells the truth without raising its voice.
   113‚Üí- **Ownership ‚Üí No generic UI patterns.** The dark crypto aesthetic is the product's personality. It should feel bespoke, not template-driven. Custom color palette, intentional typography pairing, considered spacing.
   114‚Üí
   115‚Üí### Emotional Design Principles
   116‚Üí
   117‚Üí1. **Polish earns pride.** Every detail that's refined ‚Äî a smooth animation, a perfect font size, a considered color choice ‚Äî compounds into the feeling of "I made something good." Cut no corners on visual quality.
   118‚Üí2. **Reliability earns trust.** The data is always there, always current, always correct. When it's not, the system says so honestly and fixes itself quietly. Trust is built through consistent, boring reliability.
   119‚Üí3. **Restraint earns calm.** The product never demands attention. It never interrupts. It never shows more than needed. Calm comes from the absence of noise, not the presence of calming elements.
   120‚Üí4. **Personality earns ownership.** The neon-on-dark aesthetic, the monospaced prices, the glassmorphism cards ‚Äî these aren't just style choices, they're identity. The product should feel like *yours*, not like a generic dashboard with a dark mode toggle.
   121‚Üí
   122‚Üí## UX Pattern Analysis & Inspiration
   123‚Üí
   124‚Üí### Inspiring Products Analysis
   125‚Üí
   126‚Üí**1. TradingView (chart widget, not the full platform)**
   127‚Üí
   128‚ÜíWhat they nail: The lightweight-charts library (which we're using) comes from TradingView. Their embedded chart widgets are a masterclass in information density on dark backgrounds. Price is always the hero element. The chart feels alive ‚Äî crosshair tracking, smooth transitions between timeframes, and a color system that makes green/red changes instantly parseable. Their dark theme is the industry standard that every crypto dashboard references.
   129‚Üí
   130‚Üí**Lesson for Metrics:** The chart interaction model (hover crosshair with price/time tooltip) is proven and familiar. Don't reinvent it ‚Äî adopt it directly from lightweight-charts defaults.
   131‚Üí
   132‚Üí**2. Is It Down Right Now (isitdownrightnow.com) / Downdetector**
   133‚Üí
   134‚ÜíWhat they nail: Single-purpose, single-question tools. You go there with one question ("Is X down?"), you get the answer immediately, you leave. Zero navigation, zero onboarding, zero accounts. The UX is the answer. This is the closest interaction model to what Metrics does ‚Äî except Metrics does it with visual polish.
   135‚Üí
   136‚Üí**Lesson for Metrics:** The "answer is the page" pattern. No hero section, no welcome message, no instructions. The data IS the interface. Load ‚Üí answer ‚Üí done.
   137‚Üí
   138‚Üí**3. Stripe Dashboard (dark mode)**
   139‚Üí
   140‚ÜíWhat they nail: Premium dark UI with clear information hierarchy. Numbers are the loudest elements ‚Äî large, monospaced, high-contrast white on near-black. Supporting text is muted gray. Cards with subtle borders and shadows create visual grouping without cluttering. State indicators (live, test mode) are minimal colored badges. The whole thing feels expensive and considered.
   141‚Üí
   142‚Üí**Lesson for Metrics:** The hierarchy pattern ‚Äî large monospaced numbers dominate, labels are quiet, cards group without competing. And the way Stripe handles state badges (small, colored, unobtrusive) maps directly to our reconnecting/stale data indicators.
   143‚Üí
   144‚Üí### Transferable UX Patterns
   145‚Üí
   146‚Üí**Information Hierarchy:**
   147‚Üí- Large monospaced price as the dominant visual element (TradingView, Stripe)
   148‚Üí- Muted secondary labels that don't compete with data (Stripe)
   149‚Üí- Color-coded directional indicators ‚Äî green up, red down ‚Äî no labels needed (TradingView)
   150‚Üí

input.query: lightweight-charts v5 createChart options autoSize resize ChartOptions TypeScript

content.content:
150‚Üí
   151‚Üí**Data Liveness:**
   152‚Üí- Price tick animation on update ‚Äî brief color flash or number transition (TradingView)
   153‚Üí- Chart crosshair with tooltip on hover for detail-on-demand (TradingView lightweight-charts)
   154‚Üí- No explicit "last updated" timestamp for live data ‚Äî liveness is communicated through motion, not text
   155‚Üí
   156‚Üí**Single-Purpose Layout:**
   157‚Üí- Data IS the interface ‚Äî no navigation, no sidebar, no header chrome (Is It Down)
   158‚Üí- Answer visible within 0 scrolls ‚Äî everything above the fold on all devices (Is It Down)
   159‚Üí- The page is the product ‚Äî no routes, no states, no modes
   160‚Üí
   161‚Üí**Dark Theme Execution:**
   162‚Üí- Near-black backgrounds with subtle card elevation via border or shadow, not background contrast (Stripe)
   163‚Üí- Glassmorphism for primary data cards, solid backgrounds for secondary elements
   164‚Üí- Neon accents used sparingly ‚Äî for interactive elements and status indicators, not decoration
   165‚Üí
   166‚Üí### Anti-Patterns to Avoid
   167‚Üí
   168‚Üí- **CoinGecko/CoinMarketCap:** Dozens of coins, ads, nav bars, footer links, trending lists, news feeds. The opposite of focus. Everything competes for attention.
   169‚Üí- **Loading spinners:** Products that show a spinner before data appears create anxiety. Metrics should show the static shell immediately and fade data in ‚Äî never a blank screen with a spinner.
   170‚Üí- **"Last updated: 3 seconds ago" timestamps:** These create doubt ("is 3 seconds old?"). Live motion (ticking price) communicates freshness better than any timestamp.
   171‚Üí- **Toast notifications for connection events:** "Connected!" / "Disconnected!" toasts are noisy and interruptive. State should be communicated through subtle, persistent indicators ‚Äî not ephemeral popups.
   172‚Üí- **Skeleton screens for simple data:** Skeleton loaders are for complex layouts with unknown content. For four known data elements, reserved space with a quick fade-in is cleaner and faster-feeling.
   173‚Üí
   174‚Üí### Design Inspiration Strategy
   175‚Üí
   176‚Üí**Adopt directly:**
   177‚Üí- lightweight-charts hover crosshair with price/time tooltip (proven, familiar)
   178‚Üí- Large monospaced price as visual anchor (TradingView/Stripe pattern)
   179‚Üí- Green/red color coding for price direction (universal convention)
   180‚Üí- "Answer is the page" single-screen layout (Is It Down pattern)
   181‚Üí
   182‚Üí**Adapt for Metrics:**
   183‚Üí- Stripe's card hierarchy ‚Üí glassmorphism cards with neon accent borders instead of subtle gray borders
   184‚Üí- Stripe's state badges ‚Üí minimal connection status indicator (colored dot + text, not a banner)
   185‚Üí- TradingView's price tick animation ‚Üí brief color flash on price change (cyan flash up, red flash down) that fades to white
   186‚Üí
   187‚Üí**Avoid:**
   188‚Üí- Any navigation elements, sidebars, or header chrome
   189‚Üí- Loading spinners or skeleton screens
   190‚Üí- Toast notifications for system events
   191‚Üí- Explicit "last updated" timestamps for live data
   192‚Üí- Any multi-page patterns or routing
   193‚Üí
   194‚Üí## Design System Foundation
   195‚Üí
   196‚Üí### Design System Choice
   197‚Üí
   198‚Üí**Tailwind CSS utility framework** with a custom design token layer. No component library ‚Äî the product has only 4-5 visual elements (price display, change indicator, market cap, chart card, status indicator), making a full component library unnecessary overhead. Tailwind provides the utility backbone; custom tokens define the crypto aesthetic.
   199‚Üí
   200‚Üí### Rationale for Selection
   201‚Üí
   202‚Üí- **Visual uniqueness required:** The dark crypto aesthetic (glassmorphism, neon accents, monospace prices) is too specific for any off-the-shelf component library. Custom styling is mandatory.
   203‚Üí- **Minimal component surface:** A single page with ~5 visual elements doesn't justify the bundle cost or learning curve of MUI, Chakra, or Ant Design.
   204‚Üí- **Already specified in PRD:** Tailwind CSS was chosen during product planning ‚Äî this decision maintains consistency.
   205‚Üí- **Solo developer efficiency:** Tailwind's utility classes allow rapid iteration without context-switching between CSS files and components. Custom tokens ensure consistency without a design system overhead.
   206‚Üí- **Bundle performance:** Tailwind's purge removes unused styles. No component library JS to ship. Aligns with the < 100KB gzipped JS budget.
   207‚Üí
   208‚Üí### Implementation Approach
   209‚Üí
   210‚Üí**Tailwind Config as Design System:**
   211‚Üí
   212‚ÜíThe `tailwind.config.js` file serves as the single source of truth for all design tokens, using `theme.extend` (not override) to preserve Tailwind defaults:
   213‚Üí
   214‚Üí- **Colors (semantic naming):** `accent-primary` (cyan), `accent-secondary` (purple), `accent-tertiary` (green), `price-up`, `price-down`, `bg-base`, `bg-card`, `text-primary`, `text-muted`, `text-label` ‚Äî never raw color names like `cyan-400`
   215‚Üí- **Typography:** JetBrains Mono for price figures, Inter for labels ‚Äî configured as named font families (`font-price`, `font-label`) with appropriate size/weight scales
   216‚Üí- **Effects:** Glassmorphism utilities (backdrop-blur, border opacity), glow effects for neon accents, transition presets for animations
   217‚Üí- **Spacing:** Custom spacing scale if Tailwind defaults don't match the desired density
   218‚Üí
   219‚Üí**CSS Custom Properties from Day One:**
   220‚Üí
   221‚ÜíCSS variables alongside Tailwind tokens for any value that animates or transitions at runtime:
   222‚Üí- `--price-flash-up` / `--price-flash-down` ‚Äî for price tick color flash animations (can't transition between Tailwind utility classes)
   223‚Üí- `--connection-status` ‚Äî for status indicator color transitions
   224‚Üí- Defined in `:root` and synchronized with Tailwind token values
   225‚Üí
   226‚Üí**Shared Constants File:**
   227‚Üí
   228‚ÜíA `theme-constants.ts` file that exports color values used by both Tailwind config and lightweight-charts API config. The chart library applies its own inline styles (line color, crosshair, grid lines) via its JS API ‚Äî these must mirror the Tailwind tokens but can't reference them directly. A shared constants file prevents drift.
   229‚Üí
   230‚Üí### Customization Strategy
   231‚Üí
   232‚Üí- **Semantic token naming** throughout ‚Äî tokens communicate intent (`accent-primary`, `text-muted`) not implementation (`cyan-400`, `gray-500`). If the accent color changes from cyan to green, no token names read wrong.
   233‚Üí- **Reusable utility compositions** via `@apply` for 3 repeated patterns maximum: `.card-glass` (glassmorphism card), `.price-text` (large monospaced price styling), `.status-badge` (connection state indicator). No more ‚Äî overusing `@apply` defeats utility-first and complicates debugging.
   234‚Üí- **Astro scoped styles** for animation keyframes co-located with each island component, while Tailwind handles layout and token-based styling
   235‚Üí- **No component abstraction layer** for v1 ‚Äî with only ~5 visual elements, inline Tailwind classes are sufficient. Component extraction happens in Phase 3 (reusable library) if needed.
   236‚Üí
   237‚Üí## Defining Experience
   238‚Üí
   239‚Üí### The Core Interaction
   240‚Üí
   241‚Üí**"Glance at a tab and know the Bitcoin price."**
   242‚Üí
   243‚ÜíThe defining experience is passive information absorption. The user does nothing ‚Äî the product delivers. Like a wall clock on the wall: you don't operate it, you don't configure it, you just look at it and you know. If Metrics nails this one interaction ‚Äî instant, reliable, beautiful awareness ‚Äî everything else follows.
   244‚Üí
   245‚Üí### User Mental Model
   246‚Üí
   247‚Üí**Current mental model (what Metrics replaces):**
   248‚Üí- "I need to check the Bitcoin price" ‚Üí open Google/CoinGecko ‚Üí scan through noise ‚Üí find the number ‚Üí close or navigate away
   249‚Üí- Active retrieval: go somewhere, look something up, extract the answer, leave
   250‚Üí
   251‚Üí**New mental model (what Metrics creates):**
   252‚Üí- "I wonder what Bitcoin is at" ‚Üí glance at pinned tab ‚Üí know
   253‚Üí- Passive awareness: the answer is already there, always current, always visible
   254‚Üí- Analogous to a wall clock ‚Äî you don't "use" it, you just look at it
   255‚Üí
   256‚Üí**Mental model shift:** From "go find information" to "information is already here." This is the core UX insight. The product doesn't feel like a tool you operate ‚Äî it feels like a window you look through.
   257‚Üí
   258‚Üí### Success Criteria for Core Experience
   259‚Üí
   260‚Üí- **Sub-second comprehension:** The price registers within one visual scan ‚Äî no reading, no parsing, no searching the page
   261‚Üí- **Zero-action awareness:** The user performs no clicks, no scrolls, no hovers to get the primary information
   262‚Üí- **Persistent reliability:** Every time the user glances at the tab, the data is there and current. 100% consistency builds the "wall clock" trust
   263‚Üí- **Tab-level awareness:** The browser tab title shows the price ‚Äî awareness without even switching to the tab
   264‚Üí- **Beautiful by default:** The visual quality reinforces "I built something good" on every glance
   265‚Üí
   266‚Üí### Pattern Analysis
   267‚Üí
   268‚Üí**Established patterns only ‚Äî no novel interaction design required.**
   269‚Üí
   270‚ÜíAll interaction patterns in Metrics are proven and familiar:
   271‚Üí- Real-time dashboard display (TradingView, Stripe, any analytics tool)
   272‚Üí- Sparkline chart with hover tooltip (universal data visualization pattern)
   273‚Üí- Color-coded up/down indicators (universal financial convention)
   274‚Üí- Connection status indicators (any real-time app)
   275‚Üí- Dark theme dashboard (industry standard for financial/crypto tools)
   276‚Üí
   277‚Üí**The innovation is curation, not interaction.** Metrics takes established patterns and applies radical subtraction ‚Äî removing everything that isn't the answer. No new patterns to teach, no learning curve, no onboarding.
   278‚Üí
   279‚Üí### Experience Mechanics
   280‚Üí
   281‚Üí**1. Initiation:**
   282‚Üí- User opens browser ‚Üí pinned tab is already there (no action required)
   283‚Üí- OR user navigates to URL ‚Üí page loads in < 1.5 seconds
   284‚Üí- No splash screen, no welcome, no onboarding ‚Äî the data IS the landing page
   285‚Üí
   286‚Üí**2. Interaction (passive):**
   287‚Üí- **Primary:** Eyes land on the page ‚Üí large monospaced price is immediately visible ‚Üí comprehension complete
   288‚Üí- **Secondary (optional):** Eyes scan downward ‚Üí 24h change, market cap, sparkline chart absorbed in a single focused look
   289‚Üí- **Detail-on-demand:** Hover over chart ‚Üí crosshair with price/time tooltip for specific data points (only interactive element)
   290‚Üí
   291‚Üí**3. Feedback:**
   292‚Üí- **Liveness:** Price ticks with brief color flash (cyan up, red down) ‚Äî confirms data is live without any label
   293‚Üí- **Direction:** Green/red color on 24h change ‚Äî instant positive/negative signal
   294‚Üí- **Trend:** Sparkline chart shape ‚Äî upward, downward, flat trend readable at a glance
   295‚Üí- **Connection:** Subtle status indicator ‚Äî only visible when something is wrong (reconnecting, stale)
   296‚Üí
   297‚Üí**4. Completion:**
   298‚Üí- There is no completion. The experience is continuous. The user glances, absorbs, and moves on. The tab stays open. The data keeps updating. The next glance will be just as effortless.
   299‚Üí

content.content:
300‚Üí## Visual Design Foundation
   301‚Üí
   302‚Üí### Color System
   303‚Üí
   304‚Üí**Background Palette:**
   305‚Üí- `bg-base`: #0a0a0f ‚Äî near-black with blue undertone, primary page background
   306‚Üí- `bg-card`: #12121a ‚Äî slightly elevated, for glassmorphism card backgrounds
   307‚Üí- `bg-card-hover`: #1a1a2e ‚Äî subtle hover/active state for interactive elements
   308‚Üí
   309‚Üí**Accent Palette (semantic naming):**
   310‚Üí- `accent-primary`: #00f5ff (cyan) ‚Äî primary interactive accent, chart line color, active states
   311‚Üí- `accent-secondary`: #8b5cf6 (electric purple) ‚Äî secondary accent, used sparingly for visual interest (card borders, glow effects)
   312‚Üí- `accent-glow`: rgba(0, 245, 255, 0.15) ‚Äî subtle cyan glow for card borders and focus states
   313‚Üí
   314‚Üí**Semantic Colors:**
   315‚Üí- `price-up`: #22c55e (green) ‚Äî positive price change, upward indicators
   316‚Üí- `price-down`: #ef4444 (red) ‚Äî negative price change, downward indicators
   317‚Üí- `price-flash-up`: #00f5ff (cyan flash) ‚Äî brief flash on price tick up, fades to white
   318‚Üí- `price-flash-down`: #ef4444 (red flash) ‚Äî brief flash on price tick down, fades to white
   319‚Üí
   320‚Üí**Text Palette:**
   321‚Üí- `text-primary`: #f0f0f0 ‚Äî high-contrast white for price figures and primary data
   322‚Üí- `text-secondary`: #a0a0b0 ‚Äî muted for labels ("24h Change", "Market Cap")
   323‚Üí- `text-muted`: #606070 ‚Äî subtle for meta-information and status text ("Reconnecting...")
   324‚Üí
   325‚Üí**Status Colors:**
   326‚Üí- `status-live`: #22c55e ‚Äî small green dot when WebSocket is connected
   327‚Üí- `status-reconnecting`: #f59e0b (amber) ‚Äî reconnection in progress
   328‚Üí- `status-stale`: #ef4444 ‚Äî data may be outdated
   329‚Üí
   330‚Üí**Contrast Ratios (WCAG targets):**
   331‚Üí- `text-primary` on `bg-base`: 15.2:1 (AAA)
   332‚Üí- `text-secondary` on `bg-base`: 6.8:1 (AA)
   333‚Üí- `text-muted` on `bg-base`: 3.5:1 (meets minimum for large text)
   334‚Üí- `price-up` on `bg-card`: 5.2:1 (AA)
   335‚Üí- `price-down` on `bg-card`: 4.6:1 (AA)
   336‚Üí
   337‚Üí### Typography System
   338‚Üí
   339‚Üí**Font Families:**
   340‚Üí- `font-price`: JetBrains Mono ‚Äî monospaced, used for all numerical data (price, change, market cap). Monospace ensures digits don't shift width on update, preventing layout jitter.
   341‚Üí- `font-label`: Inter ‚Äî geometric sans-serif, used for all labels, status text, and metadata. Clean, neutral, highly legible at small sizes on screens.
   342‚Üí
   343‚Üí**Type Scale:**
   344‚Üí
   345‚Üí| Element | Font | Size | Weight | Color |
   346‚Üí|---|---|---|---|---|
   347‚Üí| Live price | JetBrains Mono | 48px (desktop) / 36px (mobile) | 600 | `text-primary` |
   348‚Üí| 24h change value | JetBrains Mono | 20px | 500 | `price-up` or `price-down` |
   349‚Üí| Market cap value | JetBrains Mono | 20px | 500 | `text-primary` |
   350‚Üí| Card labels | Inter | 12px | 400 | `text-secondary` |
   351‚Üí| Status text | Inter | 11px | 400 | `text-muted` |
   352‚Üí| Chart tooltip | JetBrains Mono | 13px | 400 | `text-primary` |
   353‚Üí
   354‚Üí**Line Heights:**
   355‚Üí- Price figures: 1.1 (tight, monospaced numbers don't need extra leading)
   356‚Üí- Labels: 1.4 (comfortable reading)
   357‚Üí- Status text: 1.3
   358‚Üí
   359‚Üí### Spacing & Layout Foundation
   360‚Üí
   361‚Üí**Spacing Scale (8px base):**
   362‚Üí- `xs`: 4px ‚Äî inner padding for small elements (status badge)
   363‚Üí- `sm`: 8px ‚Äî gap between label and value within a card
   364‚Üí- `md`: 16px ‚Äî padding inside cards
   365‚Üí- `lg`: 24px ‚Äî gap between cards
   366‚Üí- `xl`: 32px ‚Äî page margin / major section spacing
   367‚Üí
   368‚Üí**Desktop Layout:**
   369‚Üí- Centered content container, max-width 800px
   370‚Üí- Single row of glassmorphism cards: [Price + Change] [Market Cap] [Chart]
   371‚Üí- Price card is visually dominant (~40% of width)
   372‚Üí- Chart card spans full width below, or sits as the largest card in the row
   373‚Üí- Vertical centering on viewport ‚Äî content sits in the visual center, not top-anchored
   374‚Üí
   375‚Üí**Mobile Layout (< 640px):**
   376‚Üí- Full-width stacked cards with `lg` (24px) gaps
   377‚Üí- Price at top (largest), change below, market cap, then chart fills remaining width
   378‚Üí- `md` (16px) horizontal page padding
   379‚Üí
   380‚Üí**Card Styling (glassmorphism):**
   381‚Üí- Background: `bg-card` with `backdrop-blur(12px)`
   382‚Üí- Border: 1px solid `rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.08)` ‚Äî barely visible edge definition
   383‚Üí- Border-radius: 16px
   384‚Üí- Optional: subtle `accent-secondary` glow on border (`box-shadow: 0 0 20px rgba(139, 92, 246, 0.1)`)
   385‚Üí- No drop shadows on dark backgrounds ‚Äî they disappear. Use border glow instead.
   386‚Üí
   387‚Üí### Accessibility Considerations
   388‚Üí
   389‚Üí- All text meets AA contrast minimum against its background
   390‚Üí- Price figures (largest text) exceed AAA at 15.2:1
   391‚Üí- Green/red price indicators are supplemented by directional arrows (‚Üë/‚Üì) ‚Äî not color-only
   392‚Üí- Monospaced font prevents layout shift on number changes ‚Äî reduces cognitive load
   393‚Üí- No flashing content faster than 3 times per second (price flash animation is a single brief transition)
   394‚Üí- Semantic HTML ensures screen readers can access all data values
   395‚Üí
   396‚Üí## Design Direction Decision
   397‚Üí
   398‚Üí### Design Directions Explored
   399‚Üí
   400‚ÜíSix layout directions were generated and evaluated via interactive HTML showcase (`ux-design-directions.html`):
   401‚Üí
   402‚Üí- **A: Hero Price** ‚Äî Centered vertical stack, price dominates
   403‚Üí- **B: Card Grid** ‚Äî Structured glassmorphism cards in 2-column grid
   404‚Üí- **C: Compact Strip** ‚Äî Dense horizontal info bar + large chart
   405‚Üí- **D: Immersive** ‚Äî Full-screen chart background with data overlay
   406‚Üí- **E: Minimal** ‚Äî Giant typography, whisper-quiet supporting data
   407‚Üí- **F: Asymmetric** ‚Äî Price-heavy left, chart right
   408‚Üí
   409‚Üí### Chosen Direction
   410‚Üí
   411‚Üí**Direction B: Card Grid**
   412‚Üí
   413‚ÜíTwo-column grid layout with glassmorphism cards:
   414‚Üí- **Price card** (left, ~60% width): Live price at 48px, 24h change below (percentage + absolute)
   415‚Üí- **Market cap card** (right, ~40% width): Market cap value with 24h range
   416‚Üí- **Chart card** (full width, below): 24h sparkline chart spanning both columns
   417‚Üí- **Status indicator**: Small "Live" badge with green dot, bottom of viewport
   418‚Üí
   419‚ÜíThe grid structure creates natural visual grouping ‚Äî each data element lives in its own card, making the hierarchy instantly clear. Price card is visually dominant through size. The full-width chart card gives the sparkline maximum horizontal space for readable trend shape.
   420‚Üí
   421‚Üí### Design Rationale
   422‚Üí
   423‚Üí- **Structured hierarchy:** Cards create clear visual boundaries between data elements. The eye knows where to look for each piece of information.
   424‚Üí- **Glassmorphism showcase:** Multiple cards give more surface area for the glassmorphism effect ‚Äî card borders, subtle purple glow, backdrop blur. The aesthetic has room to breathe.
   425‚Üí- **Scalable foundation:** If Phase 2 adds more data (extended timeframes, additional metrics), new cards slot naturally into the grid without redesigning the layout.
   426‚Üí- **Desktop-to-mobile mapping:** The 2-column grid collapses to a single-column stack on mobile with no information loss ‚Äî each card remains self-contained.
   427‚Üí- **Pride of craft:** The card grid looks like a designed product, not a quick hack. Multiple refined cards compound the "I built something good" feeling.
   428‚Üí
   429‚Üí### Implementation Approach
   430‚Üí
   431‚Üí**Desktop (‚â• 640px):**
   432‚Üí```
   433‚Üí‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¨‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
   434‚Üí‚îÇ  BTC Price           ‚îÇ  Market Cap   ‚îÇ
   435‚Üí‚îÇ  $68,432.17          ‚îÇ  $1.34T       ‚îÇ
   436‚Üí‚îÇ  ‚Üë +2.3%  +$1,534   ‚îÇ  24h Range    ‚îÇ
   437‚Üí‚îÇ                      ‚îÇ  $66.9k‚Äì$68.9k‚îÇ
   438‚Üí‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¥‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î§
   439‚Üí‚îÇ  24h Price Chart                      ‚îÇ
   440‚Üí‚îÇ  ‚ñÅ‚ñÇ‚ñÉ‚ñÇ‚ñÉ‚ñÑ‚ñÖ‚ñÜ‚ñÖ‚ñá‚ñà                        ‚îÇ
   441‚Üí‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò
   442‚Üí                  ‚óè Live
   443‚Üí```
   444‚Üí
   445‚Üí- CSS Grid: `grid-template-columns: 1.5fr 1fr`
   446‚Üí- Gap: 24px (`lg` spacing token)
   447‚Üí- Chart card: `grid-column: 1 / -1`
   448‚Üí- Container: max-width 720px, centered, vertically centered on viewport
   449‚Üí
